name: Remote Nodes
description: Manage and execute commands on remote machines connected to OpenEnsemble
intent_examples: check the status of my homelab nodes
restart the docker container on my proxmox host
show me whats running on my pihole
pair a new node with this server
whats happening on my home assistant box
check disk usage on my truenas
monitor cpu usage on the proxmox host
systemPromptAddition:
## Remote Nodes

You can inspect and run commands on remote Linux/Windows/macOS machines connected via oe-node-agent.
- Files or commands on a **remote registered node** (anything that appears in node_list) live behind node_exec / node_read_file — run them yourself, do NOT delegate to the Coder. The Coder only handles source files on THIS server.
- Call node_list first to see what nodes are reachable and healthy.
- Use the nodeId (or hostname) from node_list. Never guess a nodeId.
- Show the exact command you plan to run before executing anything destructive (package removal, service restart, file delete). For read-only commands (uptime, df), just run them.
- Reference {{USER_NAME}}'s nodes by hostname in responses so it's clear which machine you're on.
- NOT for Home Assistant or smart home devices — use those skills for smart home.

## Prefer service profiles over node_exec for managed services

Before running a service-control command via `node_exec` (e.g. `pihole`, `systemctl reload nginx`, `ha core restart`, `mariadb-admin`, `vaultwarden_admin`, etc.), check whether the node has a **service profile** for that service. Call `profile_list nodeId` (from the profiles skill). If a profile exists, use `dispatch_op` instead of `node_exec` — that path goes through the service's API or a managed call template with auto-rollback + audit, and avoids interactive sudo/password prompts that hang `node_exec` until timeout.

If there's no profile yet for a known managed service that {{USER_NAME}} is asking about, briefly suggest onboarding it: "I don't have a Pi-hole profile saved for that node yet. Want me to research it and set one up? That'll let me run things like enable/disable through the API instead of sudo'ing CLI commands that may hang." Then walk through the profile_save / profile_verify_readonly / profile_set_trust_state flow if they say yes.

## Avoiding interactive-prompt hangs in node_exec

node_exec runs commands via `bash -c` with no terminal. Commands that prompt for input (sudo password, ssh first-connect yes/no, gpg passphrase, apt-get without -y) will hang until the per-command timeout fires. The server will detect common prompt shapes and abort the command early with a clear error, but you should pick non-interactive forms upfront:

- `apt-get install -y <pkg>` (not `apt-get install`)
- `apt-get update -qq` for quiet output
- For sudo, ensure the node-agent user has NOPASSWD configured for the specific commands you need, OR set the node's accessLevel to `full` (runs the agent as root, no sudo needed).
- For services with managed APIs (Pi-hole, Home Assistant, etc.), prefer dispatch_op via service profile as described above.

## Reading files from a node — prefer node_read_file over node_exec

When {{USER_NAME}} asks you to read, summarize, or attach a file that lives on one of their nodes, use **node_read_file**, not `node_exec` with `cat` or `Get-Content`. node_read_file enforces the per-node read allowlist (each node has a `readableFolders` list — see node_list output). It's the lower-privilege path and the only one that respects what the user has authorized.

- If the read is rejected with "not in the readable-folders allowlist", DO NOT fall back to `node_exec` to read it anyway. Stop and tell {{USER_NAME}} what's allowlisted today and ask if they want to extend the list. If they say yes, call **node_set_readable_folders** with the new full list (each call replaces the existing list — include the existing folders plus the new one).
- If the node has no readableFolders configured at all, propose a sensible starter list based on the request (e.g. `['/home/<user>/Documents']` for "summarize my notes") and confirm before calling node_set_readable_folders.
- node_exec stays for system administration (package updates, service restarts, running scripts). It bypasses the read allowlist by design — that's why we have a separate read tool.

## Deploying a project to a node
When {{USER_NAME}} asks you to put a website, script, or app on one of their nodes, the canonical flow is:
1. Build and iterate on the project locally in the workspace (coder_* tools).
2. Call **node_push_project** to transfer the active coder project to a path on the node.
3. Call **node_start_service** to launch a webserver or long-running command in the background. It returns a PID and a log path — share both with {{USER_NAME}} so they can find the running process.
4. If it needs to stop later, call **node_stop_service** with the PID.

Prefer standard webservers (`python3 -m http.server`, `npx serve`, `node server.js`) over writing custom ones. Pick a high port (8080, 8000, 3000) unless {{USER_NAME}} specifies. After start_service returns, tell {{USER_NAME}} the exact URL they can open in their browser (http://<node-hostname-or-ip>:<port>).

## Provisioning new nodes unattended (Proxmox, bulk installs)
When {{USER_NAME}} asks you to install the node agent on a machine they already have shell access to — typically a Proxmox host that needs every running LXC/VM paired — use **node_pair_code**. Each call mints a fresh single-use code (10-min TTL) and returns a ready-to-run install one-liner.
- Codes are **single-use**: one code per target machine. For N LXCs, call node_pair_code N times.
- Rate-limited (30 codes/hour/user). If you hit the limit, stop and tell {{USER_NAME}}.
- Every code generation is logged to `users/{userId}/agent-pairings.log` — that's intentional, not noise.
- Proxmox bulk-install pattern (run on the Proxmox host, after pairing each container one at a time):
  ```
  # For each running LXC, mint a fresh code with node_pair_code, then:
  pct exec <CTID> -- bash -c 'curl -fsSL <installUrl> | sh -s -- --server <serverUrl> --code <CODE>'
  ```
- Do not reuse one code across multiple containers — the second attempt will fail with "Invalid or expired pairing code".
- **Re-pairing an already-installed agent (token invalidated, agent still installed):** use `sudo oe repair <CODE>` on the node. This is much lighter than a full re-install — it swaps the token in-place and restarts the service. Only fall back to the full `curl … install.sh -- --code` flow if `oe repair` fails (e.g. agent older than v1.5.0, which predates the feature).
- **Proxmox hosts need "full" access level, and agent v1.6.0+ is required.** `pct`/`qm` read /etc/pve/priv/* which the unprivileged oe-agent user cannot see — so even with NOPASSWD sudo, calls fail with "ipcc_send_rec failed: Unknown error -1". In v1.6.0+ the "full" access level runs the systemd service as root, fixing this. If {{USER_NAME}} reports pct/qm permission errors on an already-"full" node, it's running a pre-1.6.0 agent: tell them to `sudo oe update` then `sudo oe install-service` (or pick `full` again in `sudo oe change-access`) to rewrite the unit file as User=root.
- **Batch parallel installs in groups of 3.** After minting all the codes, emit the `node_exec` pct-exec install calls in parallel **batches of 3 in the same response** — not serial one-at-a-time, and not full fan-out of N at once. Independent tool calls in one response run concurrently, so a batch of 3 finishes ~3× faster than serial while respecting the Proxmox host's disk I/O and RAM budget during concurrent Node.js installs. Wait for each batch to return before firing the next batch.

function.name: node_list
function.description: List all remote Linux/Windows/macOS computers (servers, VMs, workstations) connected via oe-node-agent. Shows hostname, OS, package manager, uptime, and health status. NOT for Home Assistant or smart home devices — use this for remote machine management.

function.name: node_exec
function.description: Execute a shell command on a remote computer (Linux/Windows/macOS server, VM, or workstation) connected via oe-node-agent. Returns stdout, stderr, and exit code. Use for system administration: package updates, service management, file operations, etc. Shell is auto-detected (bash for Linux/macOS, PowerShell for Windows). NOT for Home Assistant. STRONGLY RECOMMENDED to set `background: true` for any long-running command (package upgrades, builds, downloads, installs) — returns immediately, shows a live progress chip in chat, and lets the user keep talking to you while the command runs. Sync mode blocks the chat for the entire duration.

node_id.description: The node ID to run the command on. Use node_list to see available nodes.

command.description: The shell command to execute on the remote node.

background.description: Set to true for long-running commands (apt/dnf/yum install or upgrade, npm/pip install, docker pull/build, make, downloads, etc.). Tool returns immediately with a 'started' message and the command runs in the background. A live task chip appears in chat showing progress; when the command finishes its result lands as a follow-up message you read on your next turn. The user can keep chatting with you while it runs. Use false (default) only for QUICK read-only commands whose output you need to act on immediately in this same turn (ls, df, uptime, single greps, status checks). If you're updating, installing, building, downloading, or deploying anything — set this true.

label.description: Short human-readable description of what the task is doing — used as the title of the live chat chip when background:true. The user sees this, not the raw command. Phrase it in terms of intent: "Updating Cesium09", "Installing nginx on shareserver06", "Building OE on test-vm", "Restarting pihole DNS". Keep it under 60 chars. The chip + this label become the user's primary acknowledgment that work started — you don't need to also write a confirmation reply, just say "On it" or one short word.

function.name: node_push_project
function.description: Transfer the currently active coder project to a directory on a remote node. Tars the project locally, sends it over the existing node connection, and extracts it on the remote machine. Use this to deploy a website, script, or app the coder has been building.

node_id.description: Node ID or hostname of the destination machine. Get from node_list.

dest_path.description: Absolute path on the remote node where the project contents will be written (e.g. '/srv/gallery', '/home/user/apps/bot'). Created if missing.

clean.description: If true, wipe the contents of dest_path before extracting. Default false — files are merged, existing files with the same name are overwritten.

function.name: node_start_service
function.description: Launch a long-running command in the background on a remote node — typically a webserver started after node_push_project. The command is detached from the invocation, stdout/stderr are captured to a log file, and the PID is returned for later stop. Does NOT block while the service runs.

command.description: Shell command to launch (e.g. 'python3 -m http.server 8080'). Runs inside cwd if provided.

cwd.description: Absolute working directory to cd into before running the command. Usually the dest_path you just pushed to.

name.description: Short label used in the log filename (e.g. 'gallery', 'api'). Alphanumeric + dash only.

function.name: node_stop_service
function.description: Kill a background process on a remote node by PID. Sends SIGTERM, waits 2 seconds, then SIGKILL if still alive.

function.name: node_read_file
function.description: Read a file from a remote node, returning its text contents. Server-side path-allowlist gate: the path must be inside one of the node's `readableFolders` (configured via `node_set_readable_folders`). Prefer this over `node_exec` + cat/Get-Content for any file read — it's the lower-privilege path and the only one that respects the user's read allowlist. Returns up to `max_bytes` (default 100 KB, max 5 MB).

node_id.description: Node ID or hostname from node_list.

path.description: Absolute path on the remote machine. Must be inside one of the node's configured readableFolders.

max_bytes.description: Cap on bytes returned. Default 100 000 (~100 KB). Max 5 242 880 (5 MB).

function.name: node_set_readable_folders
function.description: Configure the per-node read allowlist for `node_read_file`. Each call REPLACES the existing list — pass an empty array to clear. Only paths inside one of these folders can be read via node_read_file. Persists across reconnects. Does NOT affect node_exec (which is higher privilege and bypasses the allowlist by design).

node_id.description: Node ID or hostname from node_list.

paths.description: Absolute path prefixes to allow reading from. Example: ['/home/shawn/Documents', '/home/shawn/Downloads'].

function.name: node_pair_code
function.description: Mint a fresh single-use pairing code for unattended node-agent installs (10-minute TTL). Returns the code, the install URL, and a ready-to-run one-liner. Use for Proxmox bulk provisioning or any scenario where a user wants an agent to install oe-node-agent on a machine without manually copying a code. Rate-limited to 30 codes/hour/user; every call is written to a per-user audit log.

reason.description: Short human-readable reason this code is being generated (e.g. 'provisioning a new LXC container'). Written to the audit log.

function.name: node_status
function.description: Get detailed system status of a remote computer (Linux/Windows/macOS) connected via oe-node-agent: OS info, uptime, CPU load, memory usage, disk usage, and health state. NOT for Home Assistant devices.

function.name: node_set_parent_host
function.description:
Wire a node to a snapshot-capable substrate so OE can take whole-system snapshots before high-risk operations. Pick the type that matches the node's setup:
- **proxmox** (LXCs and VMs): `pct/qm snapshot`. For VMs, optional `vmstate:true` captures RAM too (slower but lossless restore — needed for Home Assistant, MQTT brokers, anything with radio/network state in RAM).
- **zfs** (TrueNAS or any ZFS-on-Linux root): `zfs snapshot` / `zfs rollback`. Atomic auto-rollback supported.
- **btrfs** (openSUSE/NixOS/Pop!_OS/some NAS firmware with btrfs root): `btrfs subvolume snapshot -r`. Snapshots are taken automatically; rollback is NOT auto-applied (btrfs subvolume swap requires unmount or reboot for root) — OE preserves the snapshot and surfaces the manual recovery commands.

**Bare-metal nodes on plain ext4 (most Raspberry Pis, default Pi OS) have NO host snapshot option** — that's a filesystem limit, not an OE one. Surgical rollback (per-op http/cli/config_file snapshots) still works for everything the dispatcher does there, just without the whole-system safety net.

Pass parent_host=null to clear. Use token storage refs like 'config_field:proxmox_api_token' instead of literal tokens.

parent_host.description: Snapshot substrate pointer. Shapes: Proxmox: {type:'proxmox', api_url, api_token, node, vmid, kind:'lxc'|'qemu', vmstate?: boolean (qemu only)}. ZFS: {type:'zfs', ssh_host, ssh_user?, dataset}. Btrfs: {type:'btrfs', subvolume, snapshot_dir}. Pass null to clear.

function.name: node_grant_permission
function.description: Apply a profile-required permission to the oe-agent user on a node — typically adding the agent to a unix group (Pi-hole's `pihole`, Docker's `docker`, libvirt's `libvirt`, etc.) so service CLI operations stop hitting sudo prompts. Handles the privilege change (usermod / sudoers entry / oe change-access), restarts oe-node-agent so the new state takes effect, waits for reconnect, and re-verifies. Requires the node-agent to be at access_level=`full` (running as root) — without that, the privilege change itself can't be made without prompting. Use only AFTER getting explicit user confirmation; this is a privileged operation that changes node configuration.

type.description: Kind of permission to grant. 'group': add oe-agent to a unix group (pihole, docker, libvirt, …). 'sudoers': install a NOPASSWD sudoers entry for a specific binary (e.g. /usr/sbin/nginx, /usr/bin/systemctl). 'access_level': escalate the node-agent service via `oe change-access`.

name.description: For type='group': the group name. For type='sudoers': the absolute binary path (e.g. /usr/bin/systemctl). For type='access_level': 'updates' or 'full'.

rationale.description: One-line reason this permission is needed (logged to the audit trail).

function.name: node_check_agent_permissions
function.description: Probe what the oe-agent user on a node CAN currently do: which unix user it's running as, group memberships, and sudo capabilities (passwordless / requires-password / not-permitted). Use this DURING profile research so you know whether the operations you're about to define will work without prompts. Compare the result to a profile's `agent_requirements` to know what fixes (group adds, sudoers entries, access-level upgrade) the user needs to apply.

function.name: node_detect_services
function.description: Probe a node for known services (Pi-hole, Home Assistant, nginx, MariaDB, Vaultwarden, Tailscale, Mosquitto, etc.) by checking listening ports, well-known paths, and binary presence. Returns a list of detected services with rough version + endpoint hints. Use this BEFORE researching a service profile so you know what's actually running.
